text.title: Testimonial Collection Process
text.summary: Optimal timing to ask, structured question templates, follow-up sequence, and video testimonial approach.

section.title: When to Ask

rule.statement: Ask for testimonials immediately after a measurable win — not at a fixed interval after purchase.
rule.rationale: Peak satisfaction is time-limited. Users who achieved a milestone 3 weeks ago are no longer emotionally connected to the win. The moment after a first result is the highest-response moment.

list.item: After first win: fresh excitement, highest response rate
After milestone reached: quantifiable result available
After referral: customer is already advocating

section.note: Follow up twice (Day 5, Day 10) after the initial ask. Response rate increases from ~15% to ~35% with two follow-ups. Three follow-ups show diminishing returns and risk relationship damage.

section.title: Ask Questions That Produce Usable Quotes

rule.statement: Ask specific questions in the testimonial request, not open-ended ones. "What result did you achieve?" produces better copy than "How do you like the product?"
rule.rationale: Customers default to vague praise ("It's great!") when given an open field. Specific questions unlock specific results ("saved 10 hours/week") that are actually persuasive.

list.item: "What was happening before you found us?" → relatable struggle
"What specific result have you achieved?" → quantifiable proof
"What would you tell someone considering [Product]?" → direct recommendation
"What surprised you about using [Product]?" → unexpected benefit

rule.statement: Offer a specific incentive for video testimonials and provide the question list in advance. Without preparation, video testimonials are rambling and unusable.
rule.rationale: Customers feel awkward on camera without preparation. Sending the 7 questions in advance lets them prepare a clear, confident response. The incentive signals the value you place on their time.

list.item: 1. Tell me about your role and what you do.
2. What problem were you trying to solve?
5. Walk me through how you use it.
7. What would you tell someone considering [Product]?
